elements.sent-2411-001: This paper explores quadratic forms over finite fields with associated Artin-Schreier curves.
elements.sent-2411-002: Specifically, we investigate quadratic forms of $\mathbb F_{q^n /\mathbb F_q$ represented by polynomials over $\mathbb F_{q^n}$ with $q$ odd, characterizing them using certain matrices defined by coefficients of the polynomials.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, a comprehensive treatment will be given for those polynomials whose coefficients all lie in $\mathbb F_q$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Afterwards, the results on quadratic forms will be applied to get maximal and minimal Artin-Schreier curves explicitly.. Keywords.
elements.sent-2411-005: Artin-Schreier curve, finite field, polynomial, quadratic form.
elements.sent-2411-006: Given $\mathbb F_{q^n /\mathbb F_q$, an extension of a finite field of odd characteristic, and the polynomial ring $\mathbb F_{q^n [x]$, define \begin{equation}Tr(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}x^{q^i}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $\ell$ be a nonzero $q$-linear polynomial over $\mathbb F_{q^n}$ of the form \begin{equation}\ell(x)=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}a_ix^{q^i}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-008: Here a $q$-linear polynomial over $\mathbb F_{q^n}$ is also viewed as a linear endomorphism of $\mathbb F_{q^n /\mathbb F_q$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Then $\mathrm{Tr (x\ell(x))$ as a map from $\mathbb F_{q^n}$ to $\mathbb F_q$ is a quadratic form of $\mathbb F_{q^n /\mathbb F_q$ (in fact, every quadratic form of $\mathbb F_{q^n /\mathbb F_q$ can be written in this form), and it induces a homogeneous polynomial over $\mathbb F_q$ of degree $2$ in $n$ indeterminates or a zero polynomial.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Quadratic forms over finite fields have attracted significant attention in number theory, with applications in various areas including coding theory, cryptography and combinatorics.
elements.sent-2411-011: Furthermore, they are directly related to the number of rational points of some Artin-Schreier curves over finite fields.
elements.sent-2411-012: More precisely, consider the projective curve over $\mathbb F_{q^n}$ defined by \begin{equation}y^q-y-x\ell(x)\in\mathbb F_{q^n}[x,y].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-013: If the degree of $\ell$ is $q^m$, then the genus of this curve is $\frac{(q-1)q^m 2$ (see \cite[Proposition 3.7.10]{stichtenoth2009 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For the number $N$ of rational points of the above curve, the well-known Hasse-Weil bound asserts that \begin{equation}q^n+1-(q-1)q^{\frac n2+m}\le N\le q^n+1+(q-1)q^{\frac n2+m}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-015: The curve is called a maximal curve if $N$ attains the upper bound, and called a minimal curve if $N$ attains the lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-016: We have special interests in such curves for their unique properties.
elements.sent-2411-017: There has been a lot of research concerning quadratic forms over finite fields and related maximal or minimal Artin-Schreier curves.
elements.sent-2411-018: To name a few, in \cite{klapper1997cross and \cite{fitzgerald2017invariants , the quadratic forms represented by monomials are classified.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Also, one may refer to \cite{coulter2002number for those curves defined by monomials, and to \cite{anbar2014quadratic for those curves defined by polynomials with coefficients in prime fields.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Some other results can be found in \cite{ccakccak2008curves,ozbudak2011quadratic,ozbudak2012quadratic,ozbudak2016explicit,bartoli2021explicit,oliveira2023number,oliveira2023artin .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we will present a general approach in the language of quadratic forms to determine the number of rational points of those Artin-Schreier curves.
elements.sent-2411-022: Given a basis $\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_n$ of $\mathbb F_{q^n /\mathbb F_q$, for $w=w_1\alpha_1+\dots+w_n\alpha_n$ with $w_1,\dots,w_n\in\mathbb F_q$ we have \begin{equation}Tr(w\ell(w))=w_1 \cdots w_nCw_1\\\vdots w_n,\end{equation} where $C$ is an $n\times n$ matrix whose $(i,j)$ entry is $\frac12\mathrm{Tr (\alpha_i\ell(\alpha_j)+\alpha_j\ell(\alpha_i))$.}}
